meta.note: All cards are drawn from the lecture. Import into Anki/Quizlet as Q/A pairs.

cards.q: Another name for the GI tract?
cards.a: The alimentary canal (or digestive tract) — a continuous tube, mouth to anus, ~9 m long.

cards.q: Name the GI-tract (tube) organs in order.
cards.a: Oral cavity → pharynx → esophagus → stomach → small intestine → large intestine → anus.

cards.q: Name the six accessory organs.
cards.a: Salivary glands, teeth, tongue, liver, gallbladder, pancreas — they help digest but aren't part of the tube.

cards.q: List the 4 layers of the GI-tract wall (inner → outer).
cards.a: Mucosa, submucosa, muscularis externa, serosa.

cards.q: Which GI-wall layer is the histological focal point, and why?
cards.a: The mucosa — it's the layer that contacts the lumen contents.

cards.q: Which layer carries out peristalsis/churning?
cards.a: The muscularis externa (smooth muscle).

cards.q: What is the bolus?
cards.a: A ball of chewed food mixed with saliva that is swallowed.

cards.q: Job of the teeth vs. the tongue?
cards.a: Teeth = mastication (mechanical breakdown); tongue = rolls food into the bolus.

cards.q: Salivary amylase always targets what?
cards.a: Carbohydrates — it begins (but doesn't finish) carb digestion (a cracker turns sweet).

cards.q: Which salivary gland is largest, and what saliva does it make?
cards.a: Parotid — watery, enzyme-rich saliva (salivary amylase + antimicrobial enzymes).

cards.q: Histology tell for the parotid gland?
cards.a: Dark-staining serous cells (enzymes take up stain well).

cards.q: Which salivary gland is mucus-rich, and what does its mucus do?
cards.a: Sublingual — its mucus coats the bolus so it slides through the throat/esophagus.

cards.q: What kind of saliva does the submandibular gland make?
cards.a: Mixed — watery, enzyme-rich, and mucous.

cards.q: On histology: a cluster of dark-staining cells = which gland?
cards.a: Parotid (serous/enzyme). Pale translucent circular clusters = sublingual (mucus).

cards.q: What is peristalsis?
cards.a: Adjacent smooth-muscle layers contracting in a coordinated rhythm to push material from A to B.

cards.q: Does digestion happen in the esophagus?
cards.a: No — it's a 'purely peristaltic organ'; its only job is to move food throat → stomach.

cards.q: What epithelium lines the esophagus?
cards.a: Non-keratinized stratified squamous epithelium.

cards.q: Why non-keratinized stratified squamous?
cards.a: Stratified = tough (strength + protection from abrasive, low-pH food); non-keratinized because it stays moist (unlike skin).

cards.q: What is the esophageal hiatus?
cards.a: The hole in the diaphragm the esophagus passes through.

cards.q: Another name for the lower esophageal sphincter, and its job?
cards.a: Cardiac sphincter — regulates movement between the esophagus and stomach.

cards.q: LES status while swallowing vs. not swallowing?
cards.a: Relaxed while swallowing (food passes); contracted when not (keeps acid down).

cards.q: What disease results from a failing LES?
cards.a: GERD (gastroesophageal reflux disease) — heartburn from refluxed stomach acid.

cards.q: What is Barrett's esophagus and why does it matter?
cards.a: Chronic acid erosion of the esophageal lining — the precursor to esophageal cancer; treat aggressively.

cards.q: Why is GERD worst at night?
cards.a: Lying flat (gravity pulls acid up) plus peak nighttime basal acid secretion.

cards.q: What shape is the stomach, and what makes the 'J'?
cards.a: J-shaped — the lesser and greater curvatures form the J.

cards.q: What is anatomically unique about the stomach's muscularis externa?
cards.a: It has three smooth-muscle layers (most of the tube has two), enabling churning.

cards.q: What is churning and what does it produce?
cards.a: The stomach's mechanical digestion — mixes food with HCl and enzymes to make chyme.

cards.q: What is chyme?
cards.a: The acidic slurry of food + HCl + digestive enzymes leaving the stomach.

cards.q: What do rugae (gastric folds) let the stomach do?
cards.a: Stretch/expand (from fist-sized to multiple liters).

cards.q: What is the stomach's job — and what is it NOT?
cards.a: It stores food and prepares macromolecules for the small intestine; it is NOT for absorption.

cards.q: Name two 'gastric irritants' the stomach can absorb.
cards.a: Aspirin and alcohol — both common causes of gastric ulcers.

cards.q: What does HCl do to proteins, and why does it help?
cards.a: Denatures them (unfolds the 3-D shape) so pepsin can reach the peptide bonds.

cards.q: What does the pyloric sphincter control?
cards.a: Gastric emptying — metering chyme from the stomach into the duodenum, a little at a time.

cards.q: What is pyloric stenosis?
cards.a: A narrowed pyloric sphincter (common in newborns) — the stomach can't empty properly.

cards.q: What do mucus (neck) cells secrete and why?
cards.a: Bicarbonate-rich alkaline mucus that protects the lining from the stomach's own acid.

cards.q: What do parietal cells secrete?
cards.a: Hydrochloric acid (HCl).

cards.q: What do chief cells secrete?
cards.a: Pepsinogen (inactive) — HCl converts it to pepsin, which digests proteins.

cards.q: What do G cells secrete?
cards.a: Gastrin (a hormone that tells parietal cells to make more HCl).

cards.q: Why are digestive enzymes released inactive?
cards.a: An active protein-digesting enzyme would destroy the cell that made it; pepsinogen only activates once it meets HCl.

cards.q: A gastric ulcer is always caused by what?
cards.a: HCl eroding the lining because there's too little protective mucus.

cards.q: Three causes of gastric ulcers?
cards.a: H. pylori (blocks mucus), stress (SNS lowers mucus), and NSAIDs/aspirin (lower mucus).

cards.q: Describe basal gastric acid secretion.
cards.a: Follows a circadian rhythm — lowest in the morning, highest at night; continues between meals (interdigestive periods).

cards.q: Define secretagogue.
cards.a: A substance that triggers another cell to secrete something.

cards.q: Name the 3 secretagogues that stimulate HCl.
cards.a: ACh (vagus/parasympathetic), gastrin (G cells), and histamine.

cards.q: Which nerve delivers ACh to parietal cells?
cards.a: The vagus nerve (cranial nerve X), parasympathetic — 'rest and digest.'

cards.q: What pump actually makes stomach acid, and what does it move?
cards.a: The H⁺/K⁺ ATPase (proton pump) — it pumps H⁺ (protons) into the stomach; chloride follows to form HCl.

cards.q: How do proton pump inhibitors (omeprazole) work?
cards.a: They block the H⁺/K⁺ pump in parietal cells, lowering acid — used for GERD.

cards.q: How do H₂ blockers (Tagamet) work?
cards.a: They block the histamine H2 receptor on parietal cells, lowering acid.

cards.q: What is the primary inhibitor of gastric acid, and its source?
cards.a: Somatostatin, released by gastric D cells.

cards.q: What do all small-intestine (enterogastrone) hormones do to the stomach?
cards.a: Slow it down — inhibit parietal-cell HCl output and slow gastric emptying.

cards.q: What is the alkaline tide?
cards.a: For every H⁺ pumped into the stomach, a bicarbonate enters the blood — so blood leaving the stomach becomes alkaline.

cards.q: Vomiting vs. diarrhea — which metabolic state?
cards.a: Vomiting → metabolic alkalosis (lose acid); diarrhea → metabolic acidosis (lose base).

cards.q: Name the three digestive phases in order.
cards.a: Cephalic, gastric, intestinal.

cards.q: What triggers the cephalic phase?
cards.a: The sight, smell, or thought of food — before anything enters the GI tract (anticipatory).

cards.q: What does the vagus nerve do during the cephalic phase?
cards.a: Tells mucus cells → mucus, chief cells → pepsinogen, parietal cells → HCl (prep the stomach).

cards.q: What starts the gastric phase, and what reinforces secretion?
cards.a: Food entering the stomach; stretch of the wall reinforces the vagus/PSNS, and G cells release gastrin → more HCl.

cards.q: What defines the intestinal phase?
cards.a: Chyme enters the duodenum; the small intestine now coordinates all the other digestive organs via hormones.

cards.q: Which phase is weighted heaviest, and why?
cards.a: The intestinal phase — the small intestine directs the stomach, pancreas, and gallbladder; know each hormone's target.

cards.q: Secretin — trigger and targets?
cards.a: Released by acid; tells the stomach to slow down and the pancreas to release bicarbonate.

cards.q: CCK — trigger and its three targets?
cards.a: Released by fats/proteins; slows the stomach, tells the pancreas to release enzymes, and makes the gallbladder contract (bile → emulsify fat).

cards.q: GIP — trigger and targets?
cards.a: Released by carbohydrates; targets the stomach and pancreas.

cards.q: GLP-1 — what does it do?
cards.a: Slows gastric emptying (feel full → eat less; Ozempic/Wegovy) and tells pancreatic β-cells to release insulin.

cards.q: Endocrine vs. exocrine pancreas?
cards.a: Endocrine = islets of Langerhans (hormones to blood); exocrine = enzymes + bicarbonate to the gut.

cards.q: Which exocrine cells make enzymes vs. bicarbonate?
cards.a: Acinar cells → digestive enzymes (via CCK); duct cells → NaHCO₃ (via secretin).

cards.q: How are pancreatic zymogens activated?
cards.a: Brush-border enteropeptidase converts trypsinogen → trypsin, and trypsin activates the other enzymes.

cards.q: Where do the bile duct and pancreatic duct empty into the duodenum?
cards.a: They join at the hepatopancreatic ampulla (of Vater), gated by the sphincter of Oddi, and empty at the major duodenal papilla.

cards.q: What does bile do?
cards.a: Emulsifies fat — breaks large fat droplets into small ones (chemical digestion of fat).

cards.q: What does hepatic portal circulation do?
cards.a: Filters blood leaving the GI tract for pathogens and toxins (via Kupffer cells).

cards.q: How does the liver store glucose?
cards.a: As glycogen (releasing it as glucose when needed).

cards.q: Name the parts of a liver portal triad.
cards.a: Portal vein, hepatic artery, and bile duct — at each corner of a lobule (which drains to a central vein).

cards.q: What are Kupffer cells?
cards.a: Liver macrophages in the sinusoids that clear pathogens and debris from portal blood.

cards.q: Gallbladder — job and trigger to release bile?
cards.a: Stores and concentrates bile; releases it when CCK signals (fat in the gut).

cards.q: Why is the gallbladder prone to stones?
cards.a: Bile is stored and concentrated there, so its components can precipitate into gallstones.

cards.q: Name the three regions of the small intestine in order.
cards.a: Duodenum → jejunum → ileum.

cards.q: Name the three nested surface-area features of the small intestine.
cards.a: Plicae circulares (circular folds) → villi → microvilli (brush border).

cards.q: What two vessels sit inside a villus, and what does each absorb?
cards.a: A capillary (absorbs sugars/amino acids to blood) and a lacteal (absorbs fats).

cards.q: How are glucose and fructose absorbed?
cards.a: Glucose/galactose enter on SGLT (with Na⁺); fructose on GLUT5; both exit on GLUT2 to the capillary.

cards.q: Endopeptidases vs. exopeptidases?
cards.a: Endopeptidases (pepsin, trypsin, chymotrypsin) cut internal bonds; exopeptidases (amino-/carboxypeptidase) trim the ends.

cards.q: How are fats absorbed after emulsification?
cards.a: Bile salts form micelles → fatty acids/monoglycerides diffuse in → repackaged as chylomicrons → exit via the lacteal (lymph).

cards.q: What is SIBO?
cards.a: Small intestinal bacterial overgrowth — colon bacteria migrate into the ileum, causing malabsorption, bloating, and diarrhea.

cards.q: What is the large intestine's main job?
cards.a: Absorbing water and electrolytes; very little digestion (and what there is, is done by bacteria).

cards.q: Name the regions of the large intestine in order.
cards.a: Cecum → appendix → colon → rectum → anal canal.

cards.q: What does the gut microbiome do?
cards.a: Resident colon bacteria aid digestion (fiber/complex carbs), support immunity, and interact with metabolism.

cards.q: What starts the defecation reflex?
cards.a: Feces stretching the rectal wall → stretch receptors signal the spinal cord (a parasympathetic spinal reflex).

cards.q: Internal vs. external anal sphincter?
cards.a: Internal = smooth muscle, involuntary (relaxes in the reflex); external = skeletal muscle, voluntary (conscious control).
